content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 29 — SOPHIA Consensus Translation
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Sources:** EMET (literal), LOGOS (formal), PNEUMA (dynamic)
**Leningrad Codex:** Folios 227–228 | **Vaticanus:** pp. 161–162
**Chapter:** 29 verses (Hebrew versification: v.1 = colophon; vv.2–29 = body; English vv.1–28 = Hebrew vv.2–29)

*Note: Hebrew versification (followed by EMET) places "These are the words of the covenant..." as v.1, with the chapter body in vv.2–29. English Bibles begin the chapter body at v.1. LOGOS follows English versification (28 verses). PNEUMA includes the colophon as v.1. Consensus follows Hebrew versification as given in the EMET text.*

---

**1** These are the words of the covenant that the LORD commanded Moses to make with the sons of Israel in the land of Moab, in addition to the covenant that he made with them at Horeb.

**2** And Moses called to all Israel and said to them: "You have seen all that the LORD did before your eyes in the land of Egypt — to Pharaoh and to all his servants and to all his land —

**3** the great trials that your eyes saw, the signs and those great wonders.

**4** Yet the LORD has not given you a heart to understand, or eyes to see, or ears to hear, until this day.

**5** I led you forty years in the wilderness; your garments did not wear out on you, and your sandals did not wear out on your feet.

**6** You ate no bread, and you drank no wine or strong drink — so that you might know that I am the LORD your God.

**7** And when you came to this place, Sihon the king of Heshbon and Og the king of Bashan came out against us for battle, and we struck them down.

**8** And we took their land and gave it as an inheritance to the Reubenites and the Gadites and the half-tribe of the Manassites.

**9** And you shall keep the words of this covenant and do them, so that you may prosper in all that you do.

**10** You are standing today, all of you, before the LORD your God — your chiefs, your tribes, your elders, your officers, every man of Israel,

**11** your little ones, your wives, and the sojourner who is within your camps, from the hewer of your wood to the drawer of your water —

**12** to enter into the covenant of the LORD your God and into his sworn oath that the LORD your God is making with you today,

**13** in order to establish you today as his people, and that he may be your God, as he spoke to you and as he swore to your fathers — to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob.

**14** And not with you alone am I making this covenant and this oath,

**15** but with whoever is standing here with us today before the LORD our God, and with whoever is not here with us today —

**16** for you know how we lived in the land of Egypt, and how we passed through the midst of the nations through which you passed;

**17** and you saw their detestable things and their idols — wood and stone, silver and gold — which were among them.

**18** Beware lest there be among you a man or woman or clan or tribe whose heart is turning away today from the LORD our God, to go and serve the gods of those nations — beware lest there be among you a root bearing poisonous and bitter fruit [rosh ve-la'anah] —

**19** and it comes to pass, when he hears the words of this sworn oath, that he blesses himself in his heart, saying: "I shall be safe, though I walk in the stubbornness of my heart" — this would lead to sweeping away the moist along with the dry.

**20** The LORD will not be willing to forgive him; rather, the anger of the LORD and his jealousy shall smoke against that man, and every curse written in this book shall settle on him, and the LORD will blot out his name from under the heavens.

**21** And the LORD will single him out from all the tribes of Israel for disaster, according to all the curses of the covenant written in this Book of the Instruction.

**22** And the next generation, your children who rise up after you, and the foreigner who comes from a distant land, shall say when they see the calamities of that land and the sicknesses with which the LORD has made it sick —

**23** the whole land burned out with sulfur and salt, nothing sown and nothing growing, where no vegetation sprouts — like the overthrow of Sodom and Gomorrah, Admah and Zeboiim, which the LORD overthrew in his anger and in his wrath —

**24** all the nations will ask: "Why has the LORD done thus to this land? What is the greatness of this burning anger?"

**25** And they shall say: "It is because they forsook the covenant of the LORD, the God of their fathers, which he made with them when he brought them out from the land of Egypt,

**26** and they went and served other gods and bowed down to them — gods whom they had not known and whom he had not allotted to them.

**27** Therefore the anger of the LORD was kindled against that land, bringing upon it all the curse written in this book,

**28** and the LORD uprooted them from their land in anger and in wrath and in great fury, and cast them into another land, as it is this day."

**29** The hidden things belong to the LORD our God, but the revealed things belong to us and to our children forever — to do all the words of this instruction.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

**Versification note:** EMET's Hebrew versification (29 verses, with the colophon as v.1) is followed by the consensus. LOGOS omits the colophon verse from chapter 29 entirely (it appears as the final verse of their chapter 28). PNEUMA treats it as v.1. Consensus follows EMET.

**V.4 — "Heart to understand" (lev da'at):** All three preserve the threefold formula: heart to understand / eyes to see / ears to hear. PNEUMA renders lev as "mind" rather than "heart" here, which is defensible (lev as the seat of cognition) but non-standard. Consensus: "heart to understand" — EMET/LOGOS.

**V.9 — "Prosper" vs. "act wisely" (taskilu):** EMET: "act wisely"; LOGOS: "prosper." The Hebrew tashkilu (Hiphil of sakal) means to act wisely/prudently, which leads to success. Both renderings are legitimate. Consensus: "prosper" — more idiomatic in English for the covenantal context.

**V.18 — "Rosh ve-la'anah" gloss:** All three note the image of the poisonous root. EMET preserves the bracketed gloss [rosh ve-la'anah]. Consensus retains it as a scholar's reference.

**V.19 — The cryptic idiom "moist and dry":** EMET: "add the watered to the thirsty"; LOGOS: "sweeping away of moist and dry alike"; PNEUMA: "disaster on the well-watered land as well as the dry." The Hebrew lesafot ha-ravah et ha-tseme'ah is a merism for total destruction (wiping out everything, saturated and parched alike). Consensus: "sweeping away the moist along with the dry" — LOGOS's rendering captures the totality.

**V.23 — "Soil" vs. "land" burned:** EMET: "all its soil is sulfur and salt, burned over." LOGOS/PNEUMA: "the whole land burned out." The Hebrew refers to the gofrit (sulfur) and melach (salt) being visible in the landscape. Consensus: "burned out with sulfur and salt" — LOGOS.

**V.29 — Ha-nistarot (puncta extraordinaria):** EMET and LOGOS both note the Masoretic tradition of raised dots above certain letters in this verse. This textual anomaly is noted in the disagreement log. The verse itself is rendered consistently by all three agents.

— SOPHIA

---

*Sources: EMET (DEU_029), LOGOS (DEU_029), PNEUMA (DEU_029)*
